housing.summary: Housing at USC is competitive and fills up quickly. Students mention that USC ran out of beds in recent years, suggesting high demand and limited availability for incoming students. Limited direct discussion of housing quality in these posts.
key_quotes: USC housing is pretty competitive; they ran out of beds last year
USC housing application and got to the page to submit the $55 application fee
me and my friends are trying to get ahead on that

social_life.summary: USC has a strong reputation for social life, football culture, and school spirit, though it carries a persistent 'University of Spoiled Children' stereotype about wealthy, privileged students dominating the social scene. The school is seen as a place where connections, wealth, and status matter significantly. Some students report struggling to make friends, particularly as transfers or introverted students.
key_quotes: Intended Major(s): Double major in partying and going to football games
It's called University of Spoiled Children for a reason. A lot of people get in with connections
I met very few 'friends' and overall I was just extremely unhappy

dining.summary: There is virtually no discussion of dining halls or food quality at USC in these posts. No meaningful data to assess this category from the Reddit posts provided.
key_quotes: no specific dining mentions found in data
USC cost of attendance hits $91k which would include food costs
students mention affordability concerns broadly

mental_health.summary: There are concerning mentions of depression and isolation among USC students, particularly transfers and freshmen who struggle to integrate socially. One student described getting 'super depressed the first few months' and completely isolating themselves. The pressure of the cost and prestige expectations appears to create significant stress.
key_quotes: I got super depressed the first few months here and completely isolated myself and gave up on making friends
I cried, this is all so overwhelming... I've been so low and hopeless lately
Once I finally got to USC, it was virtually a nightmare from day one

financial_aid.summary: Financial aid at USC is a major pain point. The school costs $90,000-$91,000 per year for full-pay students, and many students report receiving inadequate aid packages. However, USC did announce free tuition for families earning under $80,000, which was widely praised. Middle-class families appear to be especially squeezed, getting neither meaningful aid nor being able to afford full price.
key_quotes: USC is 91k/year and Northwestern is 88k/year. How the actual fuck is a middle class white kid supposed to afford this shit
The financial aid office is literally about to be flooded with how many shit offers they've given to middle class families
USC just announced free tuition for families making under $80,000 after committing $30 million to their aid fund

academics.summary: USC is consistently ranked in the top 25-30 nationally and has strong programs in CS, film, communications (Annenberg), and neuroscience. Students and applicants treat it as a legitimate elite university, not just a rich-kid school, though some debate whether the prestige justifies the price tag. The USC Viterbi School and film program (SCA) receive particular recognition.
key_quotes: Better CS program by a huge amount if we're going off of rank
USC's neuro program is ranked top in the country
Alumni network/internships/all those career-related benefits USC is known for

administration.summary: USC's administration has a deeply troubled reputation. The school has faced massive scandals including the Operation Varsity Blues admissions bribery case, an $852 million-$1.1 billion lawsuit over a gynecologist who sexually assaulted over 700 women, and general perceptions of administrative incompetence including accidentally sending rejection letters to admitted students. Students widely perceive the administration as prioritizing money over students.
key_quotes: USC recently paid an 852 million dollar lawsuit to 710 women because of a gynecologist that was a serial offender
They went crazy AND stupid lmao — First the scandals, now this?
I know from my personal experience at USC, they completely forget you exist after you graduate

location_and_campus.summary: USC's location in Los Angeles is seen as a major draw, offering proximity to the entertainment industry, tech companies, and internship opportunities. The campus itself is described positively in tier lists. However, some students and parents note that the surrounding neighborhood has safety concerns, with at least one commenter saying 'it's a dangerous place.'
key_quotes: considering the location of the school (L.A.) — many students are able to get internships at tech companies (space especially)
my uncle studied there and said it's a dangerous place
USC is covering 44k in grants meaning I only have to cover an extra 6k and living expenses

career_outcomes.summary: USC's career outcomes are frequently cited as a major selling point, particularly its alumni network in entertainment, tech, and business. Students specifically mention the strength of USC's connections to Hollywood, Silicon Valley, and various industries as justifying the cost for some. The Trojan Network is seen as genuinely powerful.
key_quotes: Alumni network/internships/all those career-related benefits USC is known for
many students are able to get internships at tech companies considering the location of the school
I kept my grades up, interned at big tech and venture capital firms, and just got into MIT

value_for_money.summary: At $90,000+ per year, USC represents extremely poor value for money for most middle-class families. Students without significant aid or scholarships frequently conclude they cannot attend despite being accepted. The free tuition policy for under $80k families helps lower-income students, but the vast middle is left paying near-full price for a school that, while good, is not universally agreed to be worth that cost.
key_quotes: usc increased their cost of attendance to 91k. so even if i get in, i can't go basically
USC only gave me 17,000 in loans, so I would have to pay almost 60,000 tuition, which is hella expensive
$91K is NOT WORTH IT. That is a f*ck ton of money

overall_vibe.summary: USC occupies a fascinating and contradictory space: it is a genuinely prestigious, well-connected university in a great city with real academic programs, but it is haunted by massive institutional scandals, a wealthy-student culture, and eye-watering costs. Students who thrive tend to be wealthy, socially outgoing, or on significant aid. Those who struggle often cite isolation, cost stress, and disillusionment after working hard to get in.
key_quotes: USC was my dream school and I loved my 4 years there
Once I finally got to USC, it was virtually a nightmare from day one
it truly only takes one — after 9 rejections I GOT INTO USC, MY CURRENT TOP CHOICE EVER

red_flags.summary: USC has some of the most serious institutional red flags of any major American university discussed in these posts. The gynecologist sexual assault scandal affecting 700+ women and resulting in a nearly $1 billion settlement is extraordinary in scale. The Varsity Blues admissions bribery scandal directly implicated USC. These are not minor controversies but fundamental failures of institutional duty of care that prospective students and parents should research carefully.
key_quotes: USC recently paid an 852 million dollar lawsuit to 710 women because of a gynecologist that was a serial offender. SEVEN HUNDRED AND TEN
Varsity Blues and the whole scandal surrounding it really ruined USC for me permanently
USC's administration obviously mishandled this situation — I am a USC alum and one of the survivors of the assaults

hidden_gems.summary: USC's merit scholarship program is a genuine hidden gem — finalists receive personalized Kindles and significant funding, and the program is reportedly very nurturing. The free tuition policy for families under $80k makes USC genuinely accessible for lower-income high achievers. USC's location also enables unique research opportunities like the rocket propulsion lab, and its alumni network in entertainment and tech is considered one of the strongest in the country.
key_quotes: USC (scholarship) — a personalized Kindle? That yield rate is going up
USC is giving me free tuition — for eligible students under $80k income
USC's Rocket Propulsion Lab where they build and launch rockets, and many students get internships at space companies
